34: Gamaliel I (the Elder) was one of the most respected Pharisaic teachers of the early first century (Paul studied under him, Acts 22:3). The Mishnah (m. Avot Acts 1:16; m. Sanhedrin Acts 2:6) preserves traditions of his teachings. His advice — wait and see whether this movement is from God — represents the classical Pharisaic strategy of avoiding premature judgment. His two historical examples: Theudas and Judas the Galilean. Josephus (Ant. 20.97-98) places Theudas under Fadus (44-46 CE), creating a chronological problem since this Gamaliel speech is set ca. 30-32 CE. Either Luke has a different Theudas, or Josephus's Theudas is the same person misdated, or there was an earlier failed Theudas movement.
1: The Ananias and Sapphira episode (Acts 5:1-11) immediately follows the paradigmatic community sharing of Acts 4:32-37. The structural juxtaposition — community ideal (Acts 4:32-35), positive example (Acts 4:36-37 Barnabas), negative counter-example (Acts 5:1-11) — is Luke's moral-exemplary technique, common in Hellenistic historical writing (exempla / paradeigmata). Ananias (Ḥananyah — 'YHWH is gracious') and Sapphira (Shappirah — 'beautiful' in Aramaic) are Jewish names; the couple are members of the covenant community, not outsiders. Their sin is internal — covenant-community betrayal, not pagan opposition.
2: 'With his wife's full knowledge he kept back part of the money' (enosphisato apo tēs timēs syneidyias kai tēs gynaikos) — nosphizomai (to pilfer, to keep back for oneself) is the LXX word for Achan's keeping back of devoted goods at Jericho (Joshua 7:1 LXX — enosphisanto); Luke's word-choice signals that the Ananias episode is a new-Achan story. Achan's sin (withholding from ḥerem/the devoted thing) is the defining act of covenant-betrayal that disrupted Israel's conquest. The parallel frames the Jerusalem community as a new covenant body whose integrity is similarly at stake.
3: 'Satan has so filled your heart' (ti hoti eplērōsen ho Satanas tēn kardian sou) — the direct attribution of Ananias's action to Satanic influence parallels Judas's betrayal ('Satan entered into Judas,' Luke 22:3). The Satan-filling language does not remove human moral responsibility (Acts 5:4 — 'you have not lied to men but to God' confirms Ananias's culpability); it frames the deception as a manifestation of the cosmic adversary's work within the covenant community. 'To lie to the Holy Spirit' (pseusasthai se to pneuma to hagion) identifies the Spirit as the community's governing presence — lying to the community's shared fund is lying to the Spirit who inhabits the community.
4: 'It belonged to you... wasn't the money at your disposal?' — Peter's twofold rhetorical affirmation establishes that the sin was not failure to donate but deception about the donation. The property was Ananias's own before the sale and the proceeds were at his disposal after (en tē sē exousia hypērchen); no compulsion existed. The Qumran community's rule on property retention provides an illuminating contrast: the Damascus Document (CD Acts 9:13-15) mandated full disclosure of retained property but allowed staged donation over multiple years. Luke's account implies no such staged system; the Jerusalem community's sharing was voluntary and unregulated, making deception rather than retention the offense.
5: Ananias's instant death (exepsyxen — breathed out, expired) when hearing Peter's judgment parallels the immediate death of Achan's household at Jericho (Joshua 7:24-26) and Uzzah's death for touching the ark (2 Samuel 6:6-7). Luke does not speculate on the mechanism — natural, miraculous, or divine fiat — but records the fact and its effect: 'great fear seized all who heard' (phobos megas). The phobos megas formula appears at every major divine-action event in Luke-Acts (Acts 1:12; Acts 2:43; Acts 5:5, 11; Acts 19:17) as the appropriate human response to immediate divine presence and action.
6: 'Young men (neoteroi) came forward, wrapped up his body' — the neoteroi (younger men, the junior members of the community) perform the burial — the customary role of the young in Jewish burial practice. Jewish custom required burial on the day of death whenever possible (m. Sanhedrin Acts 6:5; Deuteronomy 21:23 — 'you shall not leave his body... overnight but shall bury him the same day'). The speed (no time to notify Sapphira, who arrives three hours later knowing nothing) is consistent with this practice.
7: 'About three hours later his wife came in, not knowing what had happened' — Sapphira's ignorance of her husband's death (three hours is enough time for the burial of Acts 5:6 but apparently not enough for news to reach her) provides the narrative occasion for Peter's separate test and judgment. The three-hour gap may reflect the distances involved in the Jerusalem community's spread across the city, or the community's deliberate decision not to inform her. Peter's question ('Tell me, is this the price...?') constitutes both a test and a judgment-scenario: she has an opportunity to tell the truth.
8: Peter's question ('is this the price you and Ananias got for the land?') is a test offering Sapphira the opportunity to break with her husband's deception. The couple's 'full knowledge' (syneiduias, Acts 5:2) of the deception is retested now in the husband's absence — will she maintain the lie without him? Her confirmation of the false price (nai, tosoutou — 'yes, that much') is the moment of judgment. The judicial structure (accusation, opportunity to speak, judgment, execution) mirrors formal legal proceedings.
9: 'How could you conspire to test the Spirit of the Lord?' (ti hoti synephōnēthē hymin peirasai to pneuma Kyriou) — synephōnēthē (to agree-together, to conspire) is the conspiracy-word: the couple's agreement to deceive constitutes a coordinated test of the Holy Spirit's capacity to detect falsehood. 'Testing the Spirit of the Lord' (peirasai to pneuma Kyriou) echoes Israel's testing of God in the wilderness (Exodus 17:2, 7; Numbers 14:22; Psalms 95:9; Hebrews 3:8-9) — the deliberate provocation of a known divine presence to see if it will actually act.
10: Sapphira's death 'at Peter's feet' (pros tous podas autou) exactly mirrors the location of the community's financial offerings (Acts 4:35 — 'laid at the apostles' feet'). The spatial irony is Luke's: the location of voluntary giving becomes the location of involuntary judgment. The young men's return to find her dead and their burial of her alongside her husband completes the chiastic structure: the couple who conspired together in deception die apart (three hours between the deaths) but are buried together (pros ton andra autēs — beside her husband).
11: 'Great fear seized the whole church (ekklēsia) and all who heard about these events' — this is the first use of ekklēsia in Acts (the term that will become the standard word for the Christian community worldwide). The term had both a civic sense (the democratic assembly of citizens in a Greek polis, Acts 19:39-41) and an LXX sense (the assembly/congregation of Israel, used 73x in the LXX). Luke's use here positions the Jerusalem community as both a civic assembly and the continuation of Israel's covenant assembly. The phobos megas that falls on both the church and the wider community mirrors the fear after Pentecost (Acts 2:43).
12: 'Solomon's Colonnade' (stoa Solomōnos) — the eastern portico of the outer temple court, associated with Solomon's original temple and retained in Herod's rebuilding. Josephus describes it as the oldest structure remaining from Solomon's period (Ant. 15.11.3; 20.9.7). The portico provided covered space for large gatherings and was the site of Jesus's winter teaching (John 10:23) and the early community's daily assembly. The apostles' use of the Solomon's Colonnade creates continuity with both Jesus's temple ministry and Israel's longest-standing architectural tradition.
13: 'No one else dared join them, even though they were highly regarded by the people' — the tension between being 'highly regarded' (emegalynen autous ho laos — the people were magnifying them) and no outsider 'daring to join' (tolma kollasthai autois) reflects the community's ambiguous social status after the Ananias episode. The 'fear' of Acts 5:11 had a deterrent effect on casual adherents; the price of entry (genuine commitment under divine scrutiny) was now visible. Despite this deterrent, Acts 5:14 reports continued numerical growth — a distinction between casual association and genuine membership.
14: 'More and more men and women believed in the Lord and were added to their number' — the gender-explicit listing (andres te kai gynaikes — men and women) is characteristic of Luke's careful attention to female inclusion (Luke 8:1-3; Acts 24:10-11; Acts 1:14; Acts 2:17-18; Acts 9:36-42; Acts 16:13-15; Acts 18:26). The numerical growth despite — or because of — the Ananias episode demonstrates Luke's consistent pattern: divine discipline produces community integrity, which produces credibility, which produces growth.
15: 'Peter's shadow might fall on them as he passed by' — the contact-healing through Peter's shadow has no direct OT parallel but reflects the popular understanding that dynamis (power) radiated from holy persons. Josephus mentions similar expectations attached to Elisha's bones (2 Kings 13:21 — the dead man revived when he touched Elisha's bones; Ant. 9.8.6). In the Hellenistic world, the shadows and garments of wonder-workers were attributed healing properties. Luke does not confirm that Peter's shadow itself healed (he says people 'laid out' the sick hoping for it) but records the popular behavior without theological comment.
16: 'Tormented by impure spirits' (ochloumenous hypo pneumatōn akathartōn) — the geography of the healings expands beyond Jerusalem to 'the towns around Jerusalem' (tōn peri Ierousalēm poleōn). The same movement from Jerusalem outward characterizes the mission as a whole (Acts 1:8 — Jerusalem → Judea → Samaria → ends of earth). The specific mention of 'impure spirits' alongside diseases distinguishes the apostolic healing ministry as covering both domains of human affliction: physical illness and demonic oppression. Both are healed/expelled (etherapeunto apantes — all were being healed).
17: 'The high priest and all his associates, who were members of the party of the Sadducees, were filled with jealousy' (plēsthēsan zēlou) — zēlos (zeal/jealousy) is a powerful motivator in ancient Jewish literature: the zeal of Phinehas (Numbers 25:11) and the Maccabean warriors were celebrated as righteous; but jealousy-zēlos (the destructive envy of another's success) was condemned. The high priest's party acts from zēlos — rivalry with a successful popular movement — rather than genuine theological concern. Luke's characterization is pointed: the Sadducean establishment responds to miraculous success with envy.
18: The arrest of 'the apostles' (the plural, not just Peter and John as in Acts 4, suggesting a wider arrest) and their placement in the 'public jail' (tērēsei dēmosia — public custody, as distinct from a private or temple jail) escalates the previous individual arrest (Acts 4:3). The public jail is the Roman-administered prison of Jerusalem, more severe than the Sanhedrin's own detention facilities. The escalation pattern (arrest → release → re-arrest → flogging → eventual martyrdom) follows the progression typical of Roman persecution of unauthorized religious movements.
19: The angel of the Lord who opens the prison doors (angelos Kyriou dia nyktos ēnoixen tas thyras tēs phylakēs) belongs to the 'divine rescue' genre widespread in Jewish literature (Dan 3; 6; Judith 13; 3 Macc. Acts 6:18-21) and Hellenistic romances (Euripides, Bacchae 443-444; Ovid, Metamorphoses). Luke is not borrowing the genre but deploying it as typology: the God who rescued Daniel from the lions and Israel from Egypt now rescues the apostles from the Sanhedrin's custody. The angel's instruction ('go, stand in the temple courts') redirects the apostles immediately to the scene of their previous teaching.
20: 'Tell the people all about this new life' (lalōtte... panta ta rhēmata tēs zōēs tautēs — speak all the words of this life) — zōē (life) here is eschatological life, the resurrection-life that the apostles proclaim as having begun in Jesus. The instruction to return immediately to the temple courts (rather than flee Jerusalem) is significant: the divine rescue is not an escape from the mission but an enabling of its continuation. The apostles are returned to the exact location of the confrontation to resume exactly the activity that produced the confrontation.
21: 'At daybreak they entered the temple courts, as they had been told' — the apostles' early-morning temple entry corresponds to the temple's opening hours: the gates were opened at dawn for the morning sacrifice. The Sanhedrin's simultaneous convening ('the full assembly of the elders of Israel' — synedrion kai pasan tēn gerousian tōn huiōn Israēl) for the apostles' formal trial creates the dramatic irony: the council assembles to try prisoners who are already free and preaching.
22: 'The officers did not find them there' — the empty jail parallels the empty tomb: in both cases, the expected prisoner/body is absent, guarded doors are found locked, and the first witnesses are confused by the absence (Luke 24:3-4 — 'they did not find the body... two men in gleaming clothes stood beside them'). Luke's narrative echoes are deliberate: the apostles' prison-release participates in the same resurrection pattern that their preaching proclaims.
23: 'We found the jail securely locked, with the guards standing at the doors' — the guards' detailed report emphasizes the security of the containment: the phulakē (jail) was locked (kekleismenēn en pasē asphaleia — locked with all security) and the guards were at their posts. The impossibility of ordinary escape is Luke's apologetic point: no natural explanation (bribed guards, hidden exits, complicit jailers) accounts for the empty locked cell. The report mirrors the empty-tomb apologetic in Matthew 28:11-15 (guards bribed to deny the resurrection).
24: 'The captain of the temple guard and the chief priests were at a loss (diēporoun — thoroughly perplexed/at a complete loss), wondering what this might develop into' (ti an genoito touto) — diaporeomai (to be completely at a loss) is Luke's word for the disciples' bewilderment at the resurrection (Luke 24:4) and the crowd's at Pentecost (Acts 2:12). The same theological confusion that accompanied the resurrection now accompanies the prison-release that proclaims the resurrection. The authorities who orchestrated the containment cannot explain the escape.
25: The news that the imprisoned apostles are 'standing in the temple courts teaching the people' is delivered mid-Sanhedrin session as a messenger's intrusion — the dramatic timing maximizes the council's embarrassment. The apostles' location 'in the temple courts' (en tō hierō) is the most public and religiously significant space in Jerusalem: they have returned not to a private house but to the center of Jewish religious life, teaching as if they have never left.
26: 'They did not use force, because they feared that the people might stone them' — the Sanhedrin's officers bring the apostles 'without force' (ou meta bias) because they fear the crowd (lithasthōsin — might be stoned). The threat of stoning by the people reverses the Sanhedrin's institutional power: the council that has authority to stone the apostles dare not arrest them forcefully because the crowd might stone the council. The reversal of stoning-threat is one of Luke's consistent ironies in Acts 1-7 (prior to Stephen's actual stoning).
27: The second Sanhedrin appearance is more formal than the first (Acts 4:5-22): now the 'full assembly' (pan to synedrion) is convened, the charge is more specific (Acts 5:28), and the intended punishment is death (Acts 5:33). The pattern of escalating persecution mirrors the progression in the Synoptic Passion narrative (interrogation → formal trial → execution), and Luke's structuring of the apostolic persecution as a passion-parallel is intentional.
28: 'You have filled Jerusalem with your teaching' (peplērōkate tēn Ierousalēm tēs didachēs hymōn) — the high priest's accusation is simultaneously an inadvertent compliment: the apostolic mission has saturated the city. The charge of 'bringing this man's blood upon us' (epagagein eph' hēmas to haima tou anthrōpou toutou) echoes Matthew 27:25 ('his blood be on us and our children') — the Sanhedrin fears the legal and moral consequences of having authorized the crucifixion. The refusal to name Jesus ('this man') reflects both contempt and a kind of superstitious avoidance.
29: 'We must obey God rather than human beings' (peitharchein dei theō mallon ē anthrōpois) — the formulation is structurally identical to 4:19 but now uses peitharchein (to obey authority) rather than akouein (to listen/hear). The dei (must/necessity) frames apostolic disobedience as a divine necessity: they cannot choose otherwise. Plato attributes a similar principle to Socrates (Apology 29d); Philo applies it to Jewish resistance to Caligula's demands (Legatio 196-197). The principle — human law yields to divine law when they conflict — was recognized across Hellenistic Jewish and Greek ethical traditions.
30: 'The God of our ancestors raised Jesus from the dead — whom you killed by hanging him on a tree (xylon)' — xylon (tree/wood) rather than stauros (cross) invokes Deuteronomy 21:23 ('anyone who is hung on a tree is under God's curse') — as Paul makes explicit in Galatians 3:13. The Deuteronomic curse-of-the-tree was the Sanhedrin's argument that Jesus's crucifixion proved his divine rejection. Peter's use of xylon appropriates the Deuteronomic language and reframes it: the one hung on the tree was raised by the God of the ancestors, reversing the curse's implication.
31: 'God exalted him to his own right hand as Prince (archēgos) and Saviour (sōtēr)' — archēgos (pioneer/prince/leader/founder) appears in Acts 3:15 ('author of life'), and in Hebrews 2:10 ('pioneer of salvation') and Acts 12:2 ('pioneer and perfecter of faith'). The title combines the sense of one who goes first/ahead (pioneer) with one who rules (prince/leader). Sōtēr (saviour) is the imperial title claimed by Augustus and subsequent emperors; Luke's application of it to Jesus via the Sanhedrin-speech is a counter-imperial designation. 'To give repentance and forgiveness of sins' — the purpose of the exaltation is the provision of the gifts that bring Israel back to covenant relationship.
32: 'We are witnesses of these things, and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God has given to those who obey him' — the apostolic witness (martyres) and the Spirit's witness are presented as two distinct but coordinated testimonies to the same facts. The witness-theology follows Deuteronomy 17:6 and Acts 19:15 (two or three witnesses required for legal testimony): the human witnesses (apostles) plus the divine witness (Spirit) constitute the required two-testimony basis for the resurrection claim. The condition 'to those who obey him' (tois peitharchounsin autō) uses the same peitharchein as Acts 5:29 — the Spirit is given to those who practice the apostolic obedience-to-God-rather-than-men.
33: 'They were furious and wanted to put them to death' (dieprionto kai eboulonto anelein autous) — dieprionto (were sawn-through, cut to the quick) is a vivid image for extreme internal agitation — the emotion that will also describe the Sanhedrin's reaction to Stephen (Acts 7:54 — dieprionto tais kardiais autōn). The progression from legal injunction (Acts 4:18) to flogging (Acts 5:40) to the intended death that only Gamaliel's intervention prevents mirrors the escalation that will end in Stephen's stoning. The Sanhedrin moves through available legal remedies toward extrajudicial violence.
35: Gamaliel's speech structure follows the standard Hellenistic deliberative rhetorical form: (1) directive to remove the accused from hearing (Acts 5:34), (2) statement of the principle at stake (Acts 5:35), (3) historical examples (Acts 5:36-37), (4) conclusion drawn from the examples (Acts 5:38-39). The paired historical examples (Theudas + Judas the Galilean) are the exempla central to deliberative argument. The speech's purpose — to interrupt the slide toward judicial murder — is rhetorically achieved: the Sanhedrin is persuaded to substitute flogging for execution (Acts 5:40).
36: The Theudas reference has troubled historians: Josephus places Theudas under the procuratorship of Fadus (44-46 CE; Ant. 20.5.1), which would be after Gamaliel's speech (ca. 30-35 CE) and after the 'Judas the Galilean' rebellion of 6 CE (which Gamaliel places 'after him'). The most common solutions: (1) Luke made a chronological error; (2) Luke's Theudas was a different Theudas from Josephus's; (3) Josephus's chronology has its own problems. The name Theudas (Thaddaios) was common enough that multiple figures with this name existed in the turbulent first-century Palestinian Jewish political landscape.
37: 'Judas the Galilean appeared in the days of the census' — the census of Quirinius (6 CE) and Judas the Galilean's revolt is described by Josephus (Ant. 18.1.1; 20.5.2; J.W. 2.8.1). Judas founded what Josephus calls the 'Fourth Philosophy' — a movement combining Pharisaic theology with resistance to Roman rule on the grounds that God alone was Israel's king. The movement's violent end ('he too was killed, and all his followers were scattered') matches Josephus's description. The historical reference grounds Gamaliel's argument in recognizable recent history.
38: 'If their purpose or activity is of human origin, it will fail' (ean ē ex anthrōpōn hē boulē hautē ē to ergon touto, katalythēsetai) — Gamaliel's prudential argument is not a theological endorsement of the apostles but a tactical restraint: the test of divine origin is historical durability. The argument has the structure of a Rabbinic proof-from-consequence (kal va-chomer — if A (human movements fail), then B (wait to see if this movement fails)). The argument's limitation — it could validate any movement that survives long enough — is precisely what Gamaliel does not address; it was not his concern.
39: 'If it is from God, you will not be able to stop these men; you will only find yourselves fighting against God (theomachoi)' — theomachos (god-fighter) is a Greek concept with strong negative connotations: Euripides's Bacchae presents Pentheus as the paradigmatic theomachos who fights against Dionysus and is destroyed. Jewish tradition used theomachein for Pharaoh's resistance to YHWH (Philo, Vit. Mos. 1.96). The risk of being a theomachos is the most compelling deterrent Gamaliel can invoke before the Sanhedrin — no council wants to be recorded as having fought God and lost.
40: 'They called the apostles in and had them flogged' (deirantes — having beaten/flayed) — the flogging inflicted by the Sanhedrin was the 'forty lashes minus one' (malkot) prescribed in Deuteronomy 25:3 and described in m. Makkot Acts 3:10-12. The sentence was a moderate judicial punishment within Jewish law, far short of execution (which Gamaliel had prevented). Paul will later reference receiving this punishment five times (2 Corinthians 11:24 — 'five times I received from the Jews the forty lashes minus one'). The apostles are released with both a punishment and a repeated injunction not to speak in Jesus's name.
41: 'Rejoicing because they had been counted worthy of suffering disgrace for the Name' (katēxiōthēsan atimasthēnai hyper tou onomatos) — the paradox of rejoicing at dishonor (atimia) for the sake of honor (onoma) is the martyr-theology of the beatitudes (Matthew 5:11-12 — 'blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you... rejoice and be glad') applied to actual suffering. The suffering for 'the Name' (hyper tou onomatos — literally 'for the name,' without specifying whose) is the early Christian formula for suffering on account of Jesus's identity and authority. 3 Maccabees 6:30 and 4 Maccabees use similar language for Jewish martyrs.
42: 'Day after day, in the temple courts and from house to house, they never stopped teaching and proclaiming the good news that Jesus is the Messiah' — the dual-venue ministry (temple + house) describes the early Jerusalem church's bifurcated institutional life: public, formal proclamation in the publicly accessible temple courts; intimate, family-sized instruction in private homes (kat' oikon — home by home, house by house). This pattern anticipated the eventual expulsion from the temple and the shift to house-church-only structure that became normative for Christianity outside Jerusalem within a generation.
